Living and Non-living Things.q: What are the characteristics of living things?
Living and Non-living Things.a: Need air, water and food to stay alive. Grow. Respond to changes around them. Reproduce (make young of their own kind). Die.

Living and Non-living Things.q: What are the 4 main groups of living things?
Living and Non-living Things.a: The 4 main groups are plants, animals, fungi and bacteria.
A microorganism is a living thing that cannot be seen with the naked eye and must be studied using a microscope. Examples are bacteria, some fungi (e.g. yeast) and mould.

Living and Non-living Things.q: Explain why a mouse is a living thing but a computer mouse is not.
Living and Non-living Things.a: A mouse is a living thing because it can grow, reproduce, respond to changes and die, while a computer mouse is a non-living thing because it cannot do these things by itself.
Diversity is the great variety of living and non-living things around us.

Classifying Living Things.q: Name the main groups of animals.
Classifying Living Things.a: Animals can be grouped into mammals, birds, fish, reptiles, amphibians and insects.

Classifying Living Things.q: What are the characteristics of birds?
Classifying Living Things.a: Body covered with feathers. Have a beak. Have a pair of wings. Reproduce by laying eggs.

Classifying Living Things.q: What are the characteristics of fish?
Classifying Living Things.a: Body covered with scales. Have fins to swim. Use gills to breathe in water. Reproduce by laying eggs.

Classifying Living Things.q: What are the parts of a plant and their jobs?
Classifying Living Things.a: Roots — absorb water and minerals; anchor the plant. Stem — supports the plant; transports water and food. Leaves — make food (photosynthesis). Flowers — for reproduction. Fruits — protect and contain the seeds.
Examples of fungi are mould, yeast and mushrooms.

Materials and Their Properties.a: A material refers to what an object is made of, such as wood, metal, plastic, glass, rubber, fabric or ceramic.

Materials and Their Properties.q: State the meaning of strength and how to test it.
Materials and Their Properties.a: Strength is the ability of a material to be pulled or to carry weight without breaking or tearing. Test it by pulling or loading the material. A material is strong if it does not break easily, and weak if it breaks easily.

Materials and Their Properties.q: State the meaning of flexibility and how to test it.
Materials and Their Properties.a: Flexibility is the ability of a material to bend without breaking. Test it by bending or stretching the material. A material is flexible if it bends without breaking, and stiff if it does not bend.
Transparency describes how much light passes through a material. A material can be transparent (allows most light), translucent (allows some light) or opaque (allows no light).
Waterproof means a material does not allow water to pass through or be absorbed. Some materials float on water while others sink.

Materials and Their Properties.q: Explain why glass is chosen for windows.
Materials and Their Properties.a: Glass is chosen for windows because it is strong and stiff, waterproof, and transparent so it allows most light to pass through.

States of Matter.a: Matter is anything that has mass (the amount of matter) and occupies space (volume).

States of Matter.q: What are the 3 states of matter and their properties?
States of Matter.a: Solid — has a definite shape and definite volume. Liquid — has no definite shape but a definite volume. Gas — has no definite shape and no definite volume.

States of Matter.q: What is mass and what is volume?
States of Matter.a: Mass is the amount of matter in an object. Volume is the amount of space an object occupies.

States of Water & the Water Cycle.q: What is the freezing point of water and what happens during freezing?
States of Water & the Water Cycle.a: The freezing point of water is 0°C. During freezing, liquid water loses heat and changes from liquid to solid (ice).

States of Water & the Water Cycle.q: What is the melting point of water and what happens during melting?
States of Water & the Water Cycle.a: The melting point of water is 0°C. During melting, ice gains heat and changes from solid to liquid (water).

States of Water & the Water Cycle.q: What is the boiling point of water and what happens during boiling?
States of Water & the Water Cycle.a: The boiling point of water is 100°C. During boiling, water gains heat and changes from liquid to gas (steam).
Evaporation is the change of state from a liquid to a gas that can happen at any temperature, before reaching the boiling point.

States of Water & the Water Cycle.q: What factors affect the rate of evaporation? (W.E.T.)
States of Water & the Water Cycle.a: Wind — more wind speeds up evaporation. Exposed surface area — larger area speeds it up. Temperature — higher temperature speeds it up.
Condensation is the change of state from a gas to a liquid, e.g. water vapour cooling to form water droplets or clouds.

States of Water & the Water Cycle.q: Why is water a precious resource?
States of Water & the Water Cycle.a: Water should be conserved by reducing, reusing and recycling, and protected from pollution such as littering, oil spills and chemical waste.

Life Cycles of Animals.a: A life cycle is the different stages in the life of a living thing — birth, growth, development and reproduction.

Life Cycles of Animals.q: Describe the 3-stage life cycle and give examples.
Life Cycles of Animals.a: Egg → Young (Nymph) → Adult. Examples: chicken, cockroach, grasshopper and the frog.

Life Cycles of Animals.q: Describe the 4-stage life cycle and give examples.
Life Cycles of Animals.a: Egg → Larva → Pupa → Adult. Examples: butterfly, mosquito and beetle.
Heredity is the passing on of traits from parents to their young.

Life Cycles of Plants.q: How many stages are in a flowering plant's life cycle?
Life Cycles of Plants.a: A flowering plant has three stages: seed → young plant → adult plant.

Life Cycles of Plants.q: Which grows first after a seed germinates, the root or the shoot?
Life Cycles of Plants.a: The root grows out first, before the shoot.

Life Cycles of Plants.q: When does a young plant start making its own food?
Life Cycles of Plants.a: A young plant starts making its own food after its leaves develop.
Germination is the first stage of a plant's development from a seed.

Reproduction in Plants & Humans.a: Sexual reproduction is forming a new individual through the fusion of a male and a female reproductive cell (fertilisation).
Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma.
Fertilisation is the fusion of a male reproductive cell with a female reproductive cell (e.g. inside the ovule of a plant).

Reproduction in Plants & Humans.q: Name the male and female parts of a flower.
Reproduction in Plants & Humans.a: Male: anther (produces pollen) and filament (holds the anther up). Female: stigma (receives pollen), style, ovary (becomes the fruit) and ovule (becomes the seed).

Reproduction in Plants & Humans.q: What happens to the ovule and ovary after fertilisation?
Reproduction in Plants & Humans.a: The ovule becomes the seed and the ovary becomes the fruit.

Reproduction in Plants & Humans.q: What are the steps after fertilisation in plants?
Reproduction in Plants & Humans.a: Fertilisation → seed dispersal (by animals, wind, water or explosive action) → germination → new plant.

Unit of Life — The Cell System.q: What is the smallest unit of life?
Unit of Life — The Cell System.a: The smallest unit of life is a cell.
A system is a set of two or more parts that work together to perform a function.

Unit of Life — The Cell System.q: What makes up an animal cell?
Unit of Life — The Cell System.a: An animal cell has a cell membrane, cytoplasm and nucleus.

Unit of Life — The Cell System.q: What makes up a plant cell?
Unit of Life — The Cell System.a: A plant cell has a cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus and cell wall. Some plant cells also have chloroplasts.
The cytoplasm is the jelly-like substance where cell activities take place.
The cell membrane is a thin layer surrounding the cytoplasm that controls what enters and leaves the cell.
The nucleus is the control centre of the cell. It contains information passed on from parents to their young.

Unit of Life — The Cell System.q: How are plant cells different from animal cells?
Unit of Life — The Cell System.a: Plant cells have a cell wall outside the membrane that gives the cell its shape, and some have chloroplasts (containing chlorophyll) for making food. Animal cells have neither.

Human Body Systems.q: What are the parts and function of the respiratory system?
Human Body Systems.a: Parts: nose, windpipe, lungs (and the diaphragm). Function: takes in oxygen and removes carbon dioxide (gas exchange).

Human Body Systems.q: What are the parts and function of the circulatory system?
Human Body Systems.a: Parts: heart, blood, blood vessels. Function: transports digested food, oxygen, water and waste around the body.

Human Body Systems.q: What are the parts and function of the digestive system?
Human Body Systems.a: Parts: mouth, gullet, stomach, small and large intestines, anus. Function: breaks down food into simpler substances and absorbs them.

Human Body Systems.q: What are the parts and function of the skeletal system?
Human Body Systems.a: Parts: bones and joints. Function: gives the body support, shape and protection for delicate organs.

Human Body Systems.q: What are the parts and function of the muscular system?
Human Body Systems.a: Parts: muscles. Function: works with the skeletal system to enable movement.

Human Body Systems.q: What is digestion and what is an organ?
Human Body Systems.a: Digestion is the process by which food is broken down into simpler substances. An organ is a part of the body that performs a particular function.

Transport Systems — Plants & Electricity.a: The xylem (water-carrying tube) transports water and minerals from the roots to all parts of the plant.
The phloem (food-carrying tube) transports food made in the leaves to all parts of the plant.
Stomata are tiny openings on the underside of leaves that allow air to enter and leave for gaseous exchange.

Transport Systems — Plants & Electricity.q: What is an electric circuit and its main parts?
Transport Systems — Plants & Electricity.a: An electric circuit is the path through which electricity flows. Main parts: battery (energy source), wires (allow current to flow), switch (controls the flow) and bulbs (components).

Transport Systems — Plants & Electricity.q: What is the difference between a series and a parallel circuit?
Transport Systems — Plants & Electricity.a: A series circuit has a single, unbranching path for electricity. A parallel circuit has multiple branching paths.

Transport Systems — Plants & Electricity.q: What is a conductor and an insulator?
Transport Systems — Plants & Electricity.a: A conductor is a material that allows electricity to flow through it (e.g. metals). An insulator is a material that does not allow electricity to flow (e.g. wood, plastic).

Forces.a: A force is a push or a pull that one object exerts on another. It can make things move, change speed, change direction or change shape.
Gravitational force pulls objects towards the Earth and gives objects their weight. It exists between objects that have mass.
Friction is a force that opposes movement between two surfaces rubbing against each other.
Elastic spring force is exerted by a stretched or compressed object as it returns to its original shape.
Magnetic force is exerted by magnets, causing attraction or repulsion depending on the poles.
Weight is the amount of gravitational force acting on an object.

Magnets.a: A magnet is an object that produces a magnetic force and has two poles (North and South).

Magnets.q: State the rules of magnetic interaction.
Magnets.a: Like poles repel (N–N, S–S). Unlike poles attract (N–S).

Magnets.q: What happens to a freely suspended magnet?
Magnets.a: A freely suspended magnet comes to rest pointing in a North–South direction.

Magnets.q: Name two ways to make a magnet.
Magnets.a: A magnet can be made by the stroke method (stroking with another magnet) or the electrical method (using an electric current).
Magnetic describes a material that can be attracted to a magnet (e.g. iron, steel).

Ecosystems & Human Impact.a: A population is a group of all the organisms of the same kind that live and reproduce in a particular place.
A community is made up of all the different populations of organisms living together and interacting in a habitat.
A habitat is a place with physical factors (e.g. pond, field, mangrove) where organisms live.
An adaptation is a special characteristic that helps an organism survive in its natural habitat (e.g. camouflage).

Ecosystems & Human Impact.q: What factors affect an organism's survival?
Ecosystems & Human Impact.a: Physical factors: temperature, light, water, type of soil. Other organisms: predators, prey, competitors and disease-causing or helpful organisms.

Ecosystems & Human Impact.q: What are the negative impacts of human activities?
Ecosystems & Human Impact.a: Deforestation — leads to soil erosion, loss of organisms and global warming. Depletion of natural resources — loss of living things, global warming. Pollution — caused by littering, chemicals and smoke; leads to acid rain, ozone thinning and global warming.

Ecosystems & Human Impact.q: What are some positive impacts humans can make?
Ecosystems & Human Impact.a: Conservation of natural resources (reducing, reusing, recycling) and reforestation.

Forms & Sources of Energy.a: Energy is required to make things work or move. It is provided by sources and can be changed from one form to another.
The Sun, wind, running water and fuel.

Forms & Sources of Energy.q: Name the active forms of energy.
Forms & Sources of Energy.a: Light — enables sight and photosynthesis. Heat — changes temperature/state. Sound — energy we can hear. Electrical — runs appliances. Kinetic — the energy of moving things.

Forms & Sources of Energy.q: Name the stored (potential) forms of energy.
Forms & Sources of Energy.a: Gravitational — stored in objects at a height. Elastic — stored in stretched or compressed bands and springs. Chemical — stored in food, batteries and fuel.
Kinetic energy is the form of energy that a moving object has.
Potential energy is stored energy that can be used and changed into other forms.

Forms & Sources of Energy.q: What is the law of conservation of energy?
Forms & Sources of Energy.a: Energy can be converted from one form to another but cannot be created or destroyed.

Heat & Light.a: Temperature is a measurement of the degree of hotness of an object or place, measured in °C.

Heat & Light.q: In which direction does heat flow?
Heat & Light.a: Heat flows from a hotter object to a colder object until both are at the same temperature.
Expansion is when something increases in size as it gains heat. Contraction is when something decreases in size as it loses heat.

Heat & Light.q: What is a good conductor and an insulator of heat?
Heat & Light.a: Good conductors allow heat to pass through easily (e.g. metals). Insulators / poor conductors do not (e.g. wood, plastic, air).
It can be reflected (bouncing off surfaces). It enables us to see objects. It can pass through transparent materials. It forms shadows when blocked by opaque objects.

The Engine of Life — Photosynthesis & Re.a: Photosynthesis is the process by which plants make food in the presence of light energy. It needs light, carbon dioxide and water, and produces food (sugar) and oxygen.
Respiration is the process in cells by which oxygen and food are used to release energy, producing carbon dioxide as well.

The Engine of Life — Photosynthesis & Re.q: How are photosynthesis and respiration linked?
The Engine of Life — Photosynthesis & Re.a: They form a cycle: photosynthesis uses carbon dioxide and gives out oxygen, while respiration uses oxygen and gives out carbon dioxide.

The Engine of Life — Photosynthesis & Re.q: Trace one energy conversion from the Sun to a running person.
The Engine of Life — Photosynthesis & Re.a: The Sun gives light energy → a plant captures it in photosynthesis and stores chemical potential energy → a person eats the plant → the person runs, converting it to kinetic energy and releasing heat when sweating.
